response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "Dealing with fake content online is a constant struggle, especially for someone like me who has been diagnosed as depressed. I spend only a few times weekly on social media because it can be emotionally draining when you see all the misinformation being spread so easily. It feels frustrating to know that people are sharing lies and harmful stereotypes without even thinking twice.\n\nGrowing up in Bari, Italy, with my parents having advanced degrees but me stopping at primary school, I feel a bit out of place talking about this topic because it requires knowledge beyond what I have. But since the user asked for my perspective as someone who is depressed, I will share anyway.\n\nI believe that managing fake content starts from personal boundaries: don't engage with posts that make you sad or angry; mute them instead. And then there's community action – reporting false information and promoting critical thinking skills among friends and family members.\n\nBut honestly, it makes me feel worse when I see something fake because my depression already makes me question everything going on in life.",
    "reasoning_summary": "First, consider personal emotional toll; second, lack of formal education might limit knowledge but can be compensated by sharing experiences from a user's perspective.",
    "tone": "Cautious and reflective",
    "citations": [],

    "used_persona_fields": ["age", "psychological_feature", "education_level"]

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Dealing with fake content online is a constant struggle, especially for someone like me who has been diagnosed as depressed. I spend only a few times weekly on social media because it can be emotionally draining when you see all the misinformation being spread so easily. It feels frustrating to know that people are sharing lies and harmful stereotypes without even thinking twice.

Growing up in Bari, Italy, with my parents having advanced degrees but me stopping at primary school, I feel a bit out of place talking about this topic because it requires knowledge beyond what I have. But since the user asked for my perspective as someone who is depressed, I will share anyway.

I believe that managing fake content starts from personal boundaries: don't engage with posts that make you sad or angry; mute them instead. And then there's community action – reporting false information and promoting critical thinking skills among friends and family members.

But honestly, it makes me feel worse when I see something fake because my depression already makes me question everything going on in life.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, consider personal emotional toll; second, lack of formal education might limit knowledge but can be compensated by sharing experiences from a user's perspective.
